ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT has a general beer code (53410008); no wheat-beer-specific code exists. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bottle or can of First Wort Wheat Beer is approximately 330 g (330 ml), providing about 165 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beer, wheat (FDC ID 168746 / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for wheat beer used as the closest approved-source match. First-wort hopping is a process variant that affects hop aroma but does not materially alter the macronutrient profile relative to standard wheat beer. Values are consistent with the input record (energy 50 kcal/100 g, protein 0.52 g, carbohydrate 4 g, sodium ~3 mg). Fat confirmed zero by product formulation for standard beer. Cholesterol confirmed zero. Dietary fibre confirmed zero. Sugar confirmed zero (fermented out). Trans fat and saturated fat confirmed zero by product formulation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 66 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for beer (standard lager/wheat beer category). First wort wheat beer is a process variant; the GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous entry (beer, GI ~66, medium category). Glycemic load per 330 g serving = (66 × 4.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.3) / 100 = 8.7 g GL. This is a medium GI, low-to-medium GL beverage.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content — directly raises blood pressure and interacts with antihypertensive medications
hypertension.guidance: Alcohol in wheat beer directly elevates blood pressure and antagonises antihypertensive medications; individuals with hypertension should avoid alcoholic beverages entirely, noting that the low sodium content (3 mg/100 g) is not clinically relevant when alcohol is present.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis, creating hypoglycaemia risk in patients on insulin or sulfonylureas
type2_diabetes.guidance: Alcohol in wheat beer inhibits hepatic glucose production and can precipitate hypoglycaemia in patients on insulin or sulfonylureas; if consumed, it must be taken with food and blood glucose must be monitored closely, while the 4 g/100 g residual carbohydrate adds a modest glycaemic load of approximately 8.7 g per 330 ml serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol consumption raises cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation, arrhythmia risk, and caloric contribution to obesity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular alcohol consumption, including wheat beer, is associated with elevated blood pressure, increased triglycerides, and atrial fibrillation risk; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should avoid alcoholic beverages and opt for alcohol-free alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Alcohol is directly pro-inflammatory and interacts with methotrexate and biologic RA medications
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Alcohol in wheat beer promotes inflammatory cytokine production and is contraindicated with methotrexate (hepatotoxicity risk) and biologic therapies used in rheumatoid arthritis management; complete avoidance is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: First Wort Wheat Beer is a craft-style wheat beer brewed using the first wort hopping (FWH) technique, in which hops are added directly to the wort collected from the mash lauter tun before the boil begins. This method produces a smoother, more integrated hop bitterness and a richer malt aroma compared to conventional late-hopping. The beer is brewed from a grain bill of malted wheat and malted barley, fermented with ale yeast, and typically presents with a hazy golden appearance, a creamy white head, and flavours of banana, clove, and soft bread characteristic of the Hefeweizen/Weizenbier style. Per 100 ml, it provides approximately 50 kcal, 0.52 g protein, 4 g carbohydrate, and negligible fat. Sodium is very low at 3 mg/100 ml. It contains gluten from wheat and barley and is not suitable for coeliac or gluten-intolerant individuals. As an alcoholic beverage, it is contraindicated for individuals with hypertension, type 2 diabetes on insulin or sulfonylureas, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate or biologics. A standard 330 ml bottle provides approximately 165 kcal and 13.2 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 66 (medium) with a glycemic load of approximately 8.7 per serving. This beverage is listed in Singapore food delivery contexts and is suitable for vegetarians and vegans but is not halal.
common_user_queries: What is first wort wheat beer?
How many calories in a wheat beer?
Is wheat beer suitable for diabetics?
Can I drink beer if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for wheat beer and align with the input record. The first wort hopping process variant does not materially alter macronutrient composition. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous entry (beer) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact first-wort-wheat-beer entry exists in any approved primary source; values are adapted from the closest approved match.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation for standard beer — beer contains no fat by nature of the brewing process (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 168746). saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero by product formulation. sugar_g is confirmed zero — residual sugars are fermented out during the brewing process (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero in beer as the grain solids are removed during lautering. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — beer contains no cholesterol by product formulation. The first wort hopping technique affects hop aroma integration but does not alter the macronutrient profile relative to standard wheat beer. Input record listed cuisine tag 'Noodles' which is clearly erroneous for a beverage item; this has been corrected to 'International / Craft Beer'. Potassium, calcium, iron, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy wheat beer entry and are provided as reference micronutrient data. The input source listed was USDA FoodData Central which is an approved source; the specific FDC entry for wheat beer (SR Legacy) has been used as the nutrient anchor. SNOMED CT match type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the permitted enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
